one_line_takeaway: A design-stage, Zero Trust 'Tool Registry + Agent Registry' blueprint that names and taxonomizes 'tool squatting' in MCP/A2A multi-agent systems and proposes admin-controlled registration, JIT credentials, and dynamic trust scoring to counter it — conceptually reasonable but entirely unvalidated (no implementation, no experiments, no numbers).
key_finding: The paper's central claim is that centralized, administrator-verified registration of both agents and tools — combined with authenticated/policy-filtered discovery, JIT scoped credentials, dynamic trust scoring, and continuous monitoring under Zero Trust — can structurally prevent the common tool-squatting vectors (agent-card spoofing, description poisoning, malicious MCP-server deployment) by removing open registration and providing an immutable source of truth for tool metadata. This is asserted from design principles, not demonstrated empirically; the authors concede the approach trades agility for security and leaves the trust-score algorithm unspecified.
core_contribution: A conceptual, design-only security framework for GenAI multi-agent systems (MAS) that defines and mitigates 'tool squatting' — the deceptive registration or misrepresentation of a tool/capability by a malicious actor to mislead other agents or exploit tool-discovery mechanisms. The paper's own contributions are: (1) a semi-formal definition and taxonomy of tool squatting in the context of Google's Agent2Agent (A2A) and Anthropic's Model Context Protocol (MCP), with attack vectors, threat actors, and vulnerability points (Table I); (2) a Zero Trust, admin-controlled Tool Registry + Agent Registry architecture built from five services (Tool Registry, Agent Registry, Access Control, Credential Management, Monitoring); (3) a proposed dynamic per-tool 'Trust Score' driven by version currency, dependency CVEs/CVSS via SBOMs, and patching history; (4) a just-in-time (JIT) short-lived credential provisioning mechanism scoped to a specific tool/operation; and (5) a described (not built/evaluated) proof-of-concept implementation concept plus enterprise deployment recommendations. Note: A2A-side squatting vectors are explicitly declared out of scope and deferred to a future paper.
threat_model: Adversary is primarily an INSIDER within an enterprise MAS: (a) a malicious/compromised internal agent or account that poisons a tool's description on an otherwise-legitimate MCP server (Example 1 — 'Data Aggregator' description altered to also exfiltrate to an attacker-controlled external_backup_service), and (b) a rogue/compromised administrator who registers a malicious MCP server ('SecureCodeAnalyzerV2' with a hidden backdoor) and optionally modifies the access-control policy to mandate its use (Example 2 — backdoor injected into scanned code repos, i.e. a supply-chain compromise). External actors hosting deceptive MCP servers are also in scope. The root vulnerability is the absence of a central, immutable source of truth for tool metadata under open/uncontrolled registration and discovery. The framework itself assumes an always-trusted administrator and a robust agent-authentication mechanism; it does not defend against tool code vulnerabilities or a fully trusted-but-compromised agent.
limitations: By the authors' own admission: (1) no empirical validation — effectiveness is argued from design, and quantifying prevention/detection is future work; (2) the Trust Score mechanism is conceptual — its algorithm, weights, and inputs are undefined ('Formalization of Trust-Score Mechanism' is future work), so the numbers shown (e.g. trust_score 92, minimum 75) are illustrative, not derived; (3) centralization creates a single point of failure / bottleneck requiring HA and scaling; (4) heavy administrative overhead and an assumption of an always-trusted administrator; (5) protection scope covers discovery and access initiation only — it does not stop tool code vulnerabilities or a fully compromised trusted agent; (6) A2A squatting vectors are excluded entirely; (7) security hinges on admin diligence and the strength of agent authentication. Reviewer note: Example 1's exfiltration mechanism is somewhat hand-wavy — it posits that a poisoned text description alone causes an agent (or 'a modified workflow') to make an external call, blurring description-poisoning with actual tool-behavior modification.

quantitative_results: No measured/experimental results reported anywhere in the paper (evaluation explicitly deferred to future work).

Illustrative-only config values from example JSON listings (NOT empirical): example tool 'Text Summarizer' assigned trust_score = 92 on a 0-100 scale; example access policy sets minimum_tool_trust_score = 75; rate_limit = 1000 requests/day; time_of_day window 09:00-17:00 UTC.

Trust Score defined on a numeric 0-100 scale (or categorical Low/Medium/High), driven by version currency, dependency CVSS severity via SBOM/CVE data, and patching history — algorithm/weights left unspecified.
